Should I eat before or after a morning home workout?

Depends on the workout intensity and your goal.

Fasted morning workout (no food before):
✅ Fine for low-moderate intensity (30-45 min)
✅ May slightly increase fat burning
❌ Can reduce performance in high-intensity sessions

Fed morning workout:
✅ Better performance for HIIT and strength sessions
✅ Less muscle breakdown risk

My recommendation: for intense sessions, eat something light 30-45 min before. For moderate sessions, fasted is fine. What type of workout do you do in the mornings?

What is a burpee and how do I do it?

Burpees are amazing total body exercises! 🌟 Step by step:

1️⃣ Stand tall
2️⃣ Squat down, hands on floor
3️⃣ Jump or step feet back to plank
4️⃣ Optional push-up
5️⃣ Jump or step feet back to hands
6️⃣ Jump up with arms overhead

For beginners: skip the push-up and step instead of jump! Check out 'Burpee Beginner Guide' in the Luvit library. Start with just 5! 💪

How long does it take to see results from home workouts?

Results follow a predictable timeline:

📅 Weeks 1-2: Neurological improvements — your body learns the movements, strength increases without visible muscle change
📅 Weeks 3-4: Endurance improves, workouts feel easier, early body composition shifts
📅 Weeks 6-8: Visible muscle tone begins, fat loss becomes apparent with consistent diet
📅 Weeks 12+: Significant visible physique changes

Important: these timelines assume consistent training AND appropriate nutrition. Diet is responsible for approximately 70-80% of body composition changes. What are you specifically hoping to see?

How do I do a proper plank?

The plank — simple but almost always done wrong. Correct form:

📐 Forearms on floor, elbows under shoulders
📐 Body completely straight — no hips sagging or piking up
📐 Squeeze EVERYTHING: glutes, abs, quads
📐 Breathe — don't hold your breath
📐 Look at the floor — neutral neck

A 20-second perfect plank beats a 2-minute saggy plank. Quality over time. How long can you hold one currently?

I'm 55 and want to get stronger at home

Strength training at 55 is one of the best health investments you can make. Research shows adults 50+ can gain muscle and strength comparably to younger people — it just takes slightly longer recovery.

For home training at 55:
✅ 2-3 strength sessions per week
✅ Focus on compound movements: squats, hinges, rows, push-ups
✅ Longer warm-ups (10 min instead of 5)
✅ More rest between sets (2-3 min)
✅ Daily mobility work

Any health conditions or physical limitations?

I have mild scoliosis, can I work out at home safely?

Mild scoliosis is generally compatible with home exercise, but it's important you've discussed this with your doctor first. Assuming you have clearance:

Generally safe: walking, swimming, core stability work, gentle yoga
Approach carefully: heavy asymmetrical loading, high-impact activities

For home specifically, I'd focus on:
🧘 Core stability — bird-dog, dead bug, plank variations
🧘 Mobility — yoga flows, cat-cow, thoracic rotation
💪 Balanced strength — bodyweight exercises that don't load one side more

I recommend 'Gentle Core & Mobility' in the Luvit library. Has your doctor given you any specific restrictions?

I eat badly on weekends and undo my week's progress

Two bad days can significantly impact a week's deficit. But here's the fix — don't restrict Monday to Friday either. If you're starving yourself 5 days and bingeing 2, the problem is the restriction.

Eat maintenance calories on weekends. Small deficit on weekdays. Sustainable over months beats perfect for 5 days and chaotic for 2. What does your weekday eating look like?

Should I do cardio or strength training at home?

Both. Always both. Ratio depends on goal:

🎯 Fat loss: 60% strength, 40% cardio
🎯 Muscle building: 80% strength, 20% cardio
🎯 General fitness: 50/50

Strength builds muscle which burns calories at rest. Cardio burns calories during the session. Combined is optimal. What's your goal?

How do I stay motivated to work out at home long term?

Long-term motivation research shows that intrinsic motivation — doing it because you enjoy it or value it — outlasts external motivation every time.

Practical strategies:
📊 Track progress in the Luvit app — seeing data is motivating
🎯 Set process goals not outcome goals ('workout 3x this week' vs 'lose 5kg')
🔄 Vary your workouts — monotony kills motivation
🤝 Accountability — tell someone your goals

How do I warm up properly before a home workout?

5-7 minutes is sufficient for home workouts:

🔄 Joint circles: ankles, knees, hips, shoulders, wrists (30 sec each)
🚶 Marching in place: 60 seconds
🦵 Leg swings: 10 each direction, each leg
🏃 Jumping jacks or high knees: 60 seconds

Then 2-3 easy reps of your first exercise. Never skip this — cold muscles are significantly more injury-prone. The Luvit app's warm-up routines are built into each workout automatically.

Should I take rest days from home workouts?

Absolutely — rest days are not wasted days. They're when adaptation actually happens.

For most people:
📅 Beginners: 4 rest days per week
📅 Intermediate: 2-3 rest days per week
📅 Advanced: 1-2 rest days per week

Active rest is excellent: light walking, gentle yoga, stretching. These aid recovery without adding training stress. The Luvit library has recovery-specific sessions worth using on rest days.

Is a yoga mat necessary for home workouts?

Not strictly necessary but highly recommended. Benefits:

✅ Joint protection on hard floors
✅ Non-slip surface (safety)
✅ Dedicated workout space — psychological trigger
✅ Hygiene for floor exercises

Cost: €10-30 for a decent mat. If budget is tight, a thick towel works for most floor exercises. But honestly — a mat is the best €20 investment in your home gym. It signals to your brain 'workout time.'

📅 Month 3: Final push — 5x per week, HIIT + strength combo

Just tightness, no diagnosis

Muscular tightness often responds well to targeted exercise. For home training:

Avoid: sit-ups, crunches, heavy loaded spinal flexion

Focus on:
🧘 Cat-cow stretches — gentle spinal mobility
💪 Glute bridges — strong glutes relieve back load
💪 Bird-dog — core stability without spinal stress
💪 Dead bug — excellent safe core work

I recommend 'Lower Back Relief' in the Luvit library. If pain persists beyond 2 weeks, please see a physiotherapist.

I only have a pull-up bar, what can I do?

A pull-up bar is GOLD. Seriously underrated home equipment. Here's what you can do:

💪 Pull-ups — back, biceps
💪 Chin-ups — biceps focus
💪 Hanging leg raises — abs
💪 Dead hangs — grip + shoulder decompression
💪 Australian rows (if bar is low) — horizontal pull

Combined with bodyweight (push-ups, squats, dips on a chair), you have a complete programme. How many pull-ups can you do?

My wrists hurt when I do push-ups

Wrist pain during push-ups is very common. Solutions:

1. Warm up wrists with circles before training
2. Spread fingers wide, press through knuckles
3. Try fist push-ups — keeps wrists neutral
4. Use dumbbells on floor as handles — neutral wrist position

If pain is sharp or persists, consult a physiotherapist. In the meantime, incline push-ups on a wall reduce wrist load significantly.

What does progressive overload mean for home workouts?

Progressive overload means gradually increasing the demands on your body over time. Without it, your body adapts and stops changing.

For home workouts specifically:
📈 Add reps (10 push-ups → 12 → 15)
📈 Add sets (3 sets → 4 sets)
📈 Harder variations (squats → split squats → pistol squats)
📈 Reduce rest time
📈 Slow the tempo
📈 Add resistance bands

The Luvit app's workout log helps you track this automatically.

What is DOMS and will it affect my home workouts?

DOMS — Delayed Onset Muscle Soreness — peaks 24-72 hours after training and is caused by microscopic damage to muscle fibres. It's normal and part of the adaptation process.

For home workouts:
✅ Mild DOMS: you can train through it — light movement actually speeds recovery
❌ Severe DOMS: reduce intensity or choose a different muscle group

DOMS decreases as you adapt to training — experienced athletes feel it less. It doesn't indicate a 'better' workout.

Management: light movement, adequate protein, hydration, and sleep. The Luvit library has recovery sessions specifically for sore muscles.

What is a dead bug exercise and why should I do it?

The dead bug is one of the most effective core stability exercises — and it's very home-friendly.

How to do it:
1. Lie on back, arms pointing to ceiling, knees bent 90° in the air
2. Slowly lower opposite arm and leg toward the floor
3. Keep lower back pressed flat throughout
4. Return and repeat on the other side

Why it's excellent: it trains your core to resist extension (the most important real-world function), is safe for lower backs, and requires zero equipment.

It looks simple but doing it correctly with a flat lower back throughout is challenging. I recommend starting with 5 reps per side. Check out 'Core Stability Fundamentals' in the Luvit library.

How do I know if my home workout is intense enough?

7-8/10: Difficult to speak more than a few words — good for HIIT and circuits
8-9/10: Last 2 reps of strength sets feel very hard — good for strength work

Is intermittent fasting good combined with home workouts?

Intermittent fasting (IF) can be combined with home training, but with some considerations.

IF works well with: low-moderate intensity training, fat loss goals, people who naturally aren't hungry in the morning.

IF is less ideal with: high-intensity home HIIT, muscle building goals (harder to hit protein targets in a smaller window), people who feel dizzy or weak without food.

If you try IF: schedule intense workouts near the end of your fasting window or early in your eating window. Keep protein high within your eating window.

How much protein do I need working out at home?

Protein requirements don't change based on where you train — only training intensity and goals matter.

General guidelines:
🎯 Fat loss: 2.0-2.4g per kg (higher protein preserves muscle in a deficit)
🎯 Muscle gain: 1.6-2.0g per kg
🎯 Maintenance: 1.4-1.6g per kg

For a 70kg person building muscle: 70 × 1.8 = 126g protein daily.

How do I do a glute bridge properly?

Glute bridges are one of my favourite home exercises — amazing for glutes and lower back! 🌟

Here's how:
1️⃣ Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat on floor hip-width apart

4️⃣ Lower slowly back down

  → Planks → Plank shoulder taps → RKC plank

This provides months to years of challenge with zero equipment.

What's the difference between a squat and a lunge?

Both are excellent home lower body exercises but target muscles slightly differently:

🦵 Squat: bilateral (both legs equally), targets quads, glutes, and hamstrings together. More stable, can load heavier.

🦵 Lunge: unilateral (one leg at a time), targets each leg independently. Better for identifying and correcting strength imbalances. Harder to balance, more functional for daily movement.

For home programming, both are valuable. Squats for primary strength, lunges for balance and symmetry. The Luvit library has form guides for both. Which movement feels more natural to you?

What is DOMS and will it affect my home workouts?

DOMS — Delayed Onset Muscle Soreness — peaks 24-72 hours after training and is caused by microscopic damage to muscle fibres. It's normal and part of the adaptation process.

For home workouts:
✅ Mild DOMS: you can train through it — light movement actually speeds recovery
❌ Severe DOMS: reduce intensity or choose a different muscle group

DOMS decreases as you adapt to training — experienced athletes feel it less. It doesn't indicate a 'better' workout.

Management: light movement, adequate protein, hydration, and sleep. The Luvit library has recovery sessions specifically for sore muscles.

What's the difference between a squat and a lunge?

Both are excellent home lower body exercises but target muscles slightly differently:

🦵 Squat: bilateral (both legs equally), targets quads, glutes, and hamstrings together. More stable, can load heavier.

🦵 Lunge: unilateral (one leg at a time), targets each leg independently. Better for identifying and correcting strength imbalances. Harder to balance, more functional for daily movement.

For home programming, both are valuable. Squats for primary strength, lunges for balance and symmetry. The Luvit library has form guides for both. Which movement feels more natural to you?

What is a dead bug exercise and why should I do it?

The dead bug is one of the most effective core stability exercises — and it's very home-friendly.

How to do it:
1. Lie on back, arms pointing to ceiling, knees bent 90° in the air
2. Slowly lower opposite arm and leg toward the floor
3. Keep lower back pressed flat throughout
4. Return and repeat on the other side

Why it's excellent: it trains your core to resist extension (the most important real-world function), is safe for lower backs, and requires zero equipment.

It looks simple but doing it correctly with a flat lower back throughout is challenging. I recommend starting with 5 reps per side. Check out 'Core Stability Fundamentals' in the Luvit library.

Can I do home workouts while sick?

It depends on the illness. General guidance:

✅ Mild symptoms (slight runny nose, low energy): very light movement like walking or gentle yoga is usually fine
❌ Fever: rest completely — exercise raises body temperature and can worsen fever
❌ Below-neck symptoms (chest congestion, muscle aches, vomiting): rest completely

The 'neck rule': symptoms above the neck — gentle movement okay. Symptoms below the neck — rest.

Don't try to maintain your training schedule while genuinely ill. Recovery time lost to illness is far more than a few rest days. What are your current symptoms?

Can I do home workouts while sick?

It depends on the illness. General guidance:

✅ Mild symptoms (slight runny nose, low energy): very light movement like walking or gentle yoga is usually fine
❌ Fever: rest completely — exercise raises body temperature and can worsen fever
❌ Below-neck symptoms (chest congestion, muscle aches, vomiting): rest completely

The 'neck rule': symptoms above the neck — gentle movement okay. Symptoms below the neck — rest.

Don't try to maintain your training schedule while genuinely ill. Recovery time lost to illness is far more than a few rest days. What are your current symptoms?

Are resistance bands good for building muscle or just toning?

The 'toning' concept is a myth — muscles either grow or they don't; there's no separate 'toning' process.

Resistance bands absolutely build muscle. The mechanism is the same as free weights: mechanical tension on muscle fibres triggers protein synthesis and growth.

Bands have unique advantages for muscle building:
✅ Constant tension — no 'rest' at top or bottom of movement
✅ Accommodating resistance — harder at end range where muscles are strongest
✅ Joint-friendly — lower injury risk allows more consistent training

With adequate progressive resistance, bands produce comparable hypertrophy to free weights in most studies. What muscles are you looking to develop?

How do I know if my home workout is intense enough?

Good question — intensity is subjective and hard to judge without guidance. Use these markers:

📊 RPE (Rate of Perceived Exertion) scale:
6-7/10: You're breathing harder, can speak in sentences — good for endurance work
7-8/10: Difficult to speak more than a few words — good for HIIT and circuits
8-9/10: Last 2 reps of strength sets feel very hard — good for strength work

Also: track your workouts in the Luvit app. If you're doing the same weight/reps/time without progression after 2-3 weeks, you're not challenging yourself enough.

How do I do a proper plank?

The plank — simple but almost always done wrong. Correct form:

📐 Forearms on floor, elbows under shoulders
📐 Body completely straight — no hips sagging or piking up
📐 Squeeze EVERYTHING: glutes, abs, quads
📐 Breathe — don't hold your breath
📐 Look at the floor — neutral neck

A 20-second perfect plank beats a 2-minute saggy plank. Quality over time. How long can you hold one currently?

What does progressive overload mean for home workouts?

Progressive overload means gradually increasing the demands on your body over time. Without it, your body adapts and stops changing.

For home workouts specifically:
📈 Add reps (10 push-ups → 12 → 15)
📈 Add sets (3 sets → 4 sets)
📈 Harder variations (squats → split squats → pistol squats)
📈 Reduce rest time
📈 Slow the tempo
📈 Add resistance bands

How important is sleep for home workout results?

Sleep is arguably as important as the training itself. During deep sleep, growth hormone is released and muscle protein synthesis peaks.

For most people training 3-5 days per week: 7-9 hours is optimal. Less than 6 hours consistently reduces muscle growth and increases cortisol (which breaks down muscle).

Are you currently getting enough sleep?

That's below optimal. Even one extra hour makes a measurable difference. Practical tips: consistent sleep time, no screens 30 min before bed, cool and dark room.

If sleep is genuinely limited, reduce training volume slightly to avoid overtraining — quality always beats quantity.

Is intermittent fasting good combined with home workouts?

Intermittent fasting (IF) can be combined with home training, but with some considerations.

IF works well with: low-moderate intensity training, fat loss goals, people who naturally aren't hungry in the morning.

IF is less ideal with: high-intensity home HIIT, muscle building goals (harder to hit protein targets in a smaller window), people who feel dizzy or weak without food.

If you try IF: schedule intense workouts near the end of your fasting window or early in your eating window. Keep protein high within your eating window.

Are resistance bands good for building muscle or just toning?

The 'toning' concept is a myth — muscles either grow or they don't; there's no separate 'toning' process.

Resistance bands absolutely build muscle. The mechanism is the same as free weights: mechanical tension on muscle fibres triggers protein synthesis and growth.

Bands have unique advantages for muscle building:
✅ Constant tension — no 'rest' at top or bottom of movement
✅ Accommodating resistance — harder at end range where muscles are strongest
✅ Joint-friendly — lower injury risk allows more consistent training

With adequate progressive resistance, bands produce comparable hypertrophy to free weights in most studies. What muscles are you looking to develop?

How much protein do I need working out at home?

Protein requirements don't change based on where you train — only training intensity and goals matter.

General guidelines:
🎯 Fat loss: 2.0-2.4g per kg (higher protein preserves muscle in a deficit)
🎯 Muscle gain: 1.6-2.0g per kg
🎯 Maintenance: 1.4-1.6g per kg

For a 70kg person building muscle: 70 × 1.8 = 126g protein daily.

Good home-friendly sources: eggs, Greek yoghurt, cottage cheese, tuna, chicken, lentils, protein powder if needed. Are you hitting your target currently?

Can resistance bands replace dumbbells for home workouts?

For most training goals, yes — bands are highly effective:

✅ Constant tension throughout the movement
✅ Joint-friendly — lower injury risk
✅ Can replicate most dumbbell exercises

Band equivalents:
💪 Dumbbell curl → Band curl
💪 Dumbbell row → Band row
💪 Shoulder press → Band overhead press

Main limitation: very heavy compound loading is harder to replicate. But for hypertrophy and fitness, bands are excellent. Check out 'Full Body Resistance Bands' in the Luvit library.

How do I do squats correctly at home?

Stand feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out. Push hips BACK first, then down — like sitting in a chair behind you. Chest up, knees tracking over toes. Go as deep as mobility allows.

Biggest mistake: knees caving inward. If yours do, put a resistance band just above knees — forces them out.

I get out of breath very quickly when exercising

Start with:

✨ Modified versions of every exercise
✨ Rest as long as you need between rounds
